coredata.dc:title: Constructing carbon quantum dots/Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic activity and mechanism investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Di, Jun
Xia, Jiexiang
Huang, Yan
Ji, Mengxia
Fan, Wenmin
Chen, Zhigang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets have been controlled synthetized. The CQDs with average diameter about 3nm was dispersed on the surface of Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets, in which the Bi2SiO5 displayed the thinnest thickness reported up to now. The ultrathin structure was designed to accelerate the carriers transfer among the Bi2SiO5 nanosheet interior while the CQDs was modified to promote the surface charge carrier separation. After the CQDs was introduced to the Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets, the photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under UV light irradiation greatly improved. Through the electron spin resonance analysis, the main active species were determined to be hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical in this system and further proved by the XPS valence spectra. The modified CQDs could boost the generation of both hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical, which subsequently increase the photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Bi2SiO5
Ultrathin nanosheets
Photocatalytic
Active species

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 samples with different CQDs contents.
FT-IR spectra of pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 samples with different CQDs contents.
XPS spectra of pure Bi2SiO5 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 samples: (a) survey of the sample (b) Bi 4f, (c) Si 2p, (d) O 1s and (e) C 1s.
TEM images of (a) Bi2SiO5 and (b and c) 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5; (d) EDS of the 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.
AFM image and the corresponding height profile of the Bi2SiO5.
The diffuse reflection spectra and photograph of the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm of (a) pure Bi2SiO5, (b) 0.5wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5, (c) 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 and (d) 2wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.
(a) Transient photocurrent response, (b) electrochemical impedance spectra (c) steady-state PL spectra and (d) time-resolved transient PL decay of pure Bi2SiO5 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.
Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials under UV light irradiation.

ESR spectra of the (a and c) DMPO-O2
− and (b and d) DMPO-
OH adducts recorded with (a and b) pure Bi2SiO5 and (c and d) 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 under UV light irradiation.
Valence-band XPS spectra of the pure Bi2SiO5 and 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.
Schematic diagram illustrating the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 under UV light irradiation along with the possible reaction mechanism.

head.ce:title: Constructing carbon quantum dots/Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic activity and mechanism investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CQDs modified Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets have been controlled synthetized. The modified CQDs could boost the generation of both hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical, which subsequently increase the photocatalytic performance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets have been controlled synthetized. The CQDs with average diameter about 3nm was dispersed on the surface of Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets, in which the Bi2SiO5 displayed the thinnest thickness reported up to now. The ultrathin structure was designed to accelerate the carriers transfer among the Bi2SiO5 nanosheet interior while the CQDs was modified to promote the surface charge carrier separation. After the CQDs was introduced to the Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets, the photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under UV light irradiation greatly improved. Through the electron spin resonance analysis, the main active species were determined to be hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical in this system and further proved by the XPS valence spectra. The modified CQDs could boost the generation of both hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical, which subsequently increase the photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) materials have attracted substantial research interest recent years due to their fascinating properties and enormous applicability in areas of electronics, catalysis, photovoltaics, energy storage devices and so on [1–4]. Compared with the corresponding bulk materials, the ultrathin materials with atomically thickness would display the variation of atomic structures such as coordination number, bond length, and degree of atom disorder which would subsequently affect their physicochemical properties [5]. For example, unlike bulk boron allotropes, borophene shows metallic characteristics that are consistent with predictions of a highly anisotropic, 2D metal [6]. The single-unit-cell CoSe2 sheets displayed greatly enhanced electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction efficiency compared to the bulk CoSe2 [7]. And the graphene-like BN exhibited the 16.8 times higher adsorptive desulfurization capacity than bulk counterparts [8].

Recently, Bi2SiO5 has drawn some attentions owning to its nontoxicity, high stability and good photocatalytic activity as a new promising layered material [9,10]. As a typical Aurivillius oxide, Bi2SiO5 was built up with [Bi2O2]2+ layers sandwiched between SiO3 2− pyroxene file slabs [11]. Up to now, several Bi2SiO5 materials with different morphologies such as nanoparticles [12], nanosheets [13], mesoporous microspheres [14] and sponge-like hierarchical nanostructure [15] have been obtained. However, no ultrathin nanosheet structure was controlled prepared up to now. When the thickness of bulk materials reduced to atomic thickness, largely low-coordinated surface atoms can be exposed. These low-coordinated surface atoms with more dangling bonds could harvest more light [16]. At the same time, the photogenerated charge carriers, which produced within the interior of the semiconductors, would take a longer time to arrive the surface when compared with those carriers generated approach surface. Thus, the charge carriers generated in the bulk materials were more likely to be lost owning to the recombination and the ultrathin nanosheets structure could enable strikingly fast charge carriers transfer and effective separation [17]. Intuitively, it was desirable to employ appropriate strategy to prepare Bi2SiO5 nanosheets with ultrathin thickness.

The charge separation was the most complex and key factor essentially determining the photocatalytic efficiency. Generally, the recombination process of electron-hole pairs included two aspects, named bulk phase recombination and surface recombination [18]. Although the bulk phase separation has been accelerated by the ultrathin structure, the surface high recombination rate of charge carriers would also limit the photocatalytic activity owning to the sluggish superficial redox reactions. Thus, it was necessary to further build surface separation centers to take full advantage of the charge carriers and then accelerate the superficial redox reactions. As a novel class of recently discovered carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has attracted massive research interest owning to the fascinating physical and chemical properties [19]. It has been introduced to numerous applications in areas ranging from electronics to catalysis and others, due to its superior biocompatibility, high stability, low cost, electron-accepting and -transport properties [20]. The CQDs has been applied in the photocatalysis systems recent years [21–27]. Considering the fact of small sized CQDs and ultrathin structure Bi2SiO5, the accessible area between the CQDs and Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets can be greatly increased, and thus the nanoscale junctions with closely contacts can be built. The photogenerated charge carriers could transport rapidly from inside to the surface and then transfer across the interface to the CQDs. As a result, the efficient charge separation of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets may be acquired.

Herein, the CQDs modified Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets has been prepared for the first time, in which the Bi2SiO5 displayed the thinnest thickness reported up to now. The structures, morphologies, optical properties and photoelectrical properties were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic performance of the obtained CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials was evaluated for the degradation of RhB under UV light irradiation. The main active species was determined via electron spin resonance analysis and further proved by the XPS valence spectra. The relationship between the structure and the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials was also discussed in detail.
All chemical reagents were purchased and used without further purification. The CQDs solid was prepared according to the literature followed by the freeze-drying process [28].

1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 10mL ethylene glycol to obtain solution A. 1mmol Na2SiO3·9H2O was dispersed in 10mL H2O to obtain solution B. The solution A was added into solution B under stirring and further stirred for 30min. Then the mixing solution was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated to 180°C for 24h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried. Subsequently, the above powder was calcined at 500°C for 12h within the tube furnace. Then, 0.1g calcined product and a certain amount of CQDs was dispersed into 10mL H2O, stirred and ultrasonic for 30min. After centrifugation, the sample was washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times, and the product was dried under vacuum at 50°C for 24h. The obtained materials was denoted as X wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5, where X wt% was the initial weight ratio of CQDs.
Bi2SiO5 microsphere was prepared according to the literature [11].

The crystal structure of samples was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer) using Cu Kα irradiation. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed on CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg Kα source. Raman spectra were recorded on a micro Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Invia) in a backscattering geometry with a 532nm laser as an excitation source. The morphologies of the samples were observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7001F) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100). BET surface area measurements were carried out by N2 adsorption at 77K on a TriStar II 3020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer (Micromeritics). The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS, taking BaSO4 as internal reference sample) were performed using a UV-2450 (Shimadzu) spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere assembly. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the prepared samples were detected using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were performed on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer by using the spin-trap reagent DMPO in water (for hydroxyl radical) and methanol (for superoxide radical). The electrochemical experimental results were carried out using a CHI 660B electrochemical system with a three electrode system. The thin film of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials on ITO was used as the working electrodes, the Pt wire was employed as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl/sat. KCl was utilized as a reference electrode. The photocurrent and EIS were performed in the phosphate buffer solution (0.1M, pH 7.0), and 0.1M KCl solution with 5mM Fe(CN)6 3 −/Fe(CN)6 4 −, respectively.

The photocatalytic performance was evaluated by the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and phenol with initial concentration of 10mgL−1. A 250W Hg lamp was employed as UV light irradiation. More specifically, 20 and 100mg photocatalyst samples were added to 100mL of RhB and phenol solution, respectively. Before irradiation, to achieve adsorptive equilibrium, suspensions were mixed under vigorous stirring in the dark for 30min. Aeration was conducted using an air pump to ensure a constant supply of oxygen. After certain time intervals, 3mL suspension was sampled and centrifuged to separate the supernatant liquid from photocatalysts. The concentration of RhB and phenol was determined by an UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu) according to its absorbance wavelength at 553 and 270nm respectively.

The structural information of the obtained materials was revealed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis [29]. Fig. 1 displayed the XRD pattern of the Bi2SiO5, 0.5wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5, 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 and 2wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials. The diffraction peaks centered at 2θ =11.6°, 23.9°, 29.2°, 32.6°, 35.3°, 41.4°, 47.6°, and 49.6° can be indexed to the (200), (310), (311), (020), (600), (402), (022) and (602) crystal planes of orthorhombic phase of Bi2SiO5 (JCPDS Card 36-0287) [9]. However, no diffraction peaks corresponding to the CQDs can be detected in the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials, which may be ascribed to the low content of CQDs. No other peaks from possible impurities such as Bi2O3, Bi4Si3O12, or Bi12SiO20 were detected, revealing the high purity of the as-prepared samples.

The FT-IR analysis was employed to determine the existence of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials and the result was shown in Fig. 2 . For the pure Bi2SiO5, the band around 447cm−1 was ascribed to the BiO stretching mode [30]. The absorption peak at 1629cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibrations of OH, which was derived from the adsorbed water. The absorption band centered at 862cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration mode of BiOSi bonds. The absorption bands at 951cm−1 and 1043cm−1 were ascribed to stretching vibration mode of isolated (SiO5)6 − groups and stretching vibration mode of the SiO bond, respectively [11]. The above result suggested the Bi2SiO5 has been prepared successfully. With respect to the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials, the bands at 1532 and 1699cm−1 were attributed to bending vibrations of NH and vibrational absorption band of CO, respectively, which derived from the CQDs [28,31]. The result of FT-IR analysis implied the CQDs has been introduced to the Bi2SiO5 successfully.

The surface chemical composition and chemical state of the pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials was analyzed via XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Bi, Si, O and C elements can be observed in the survey scan spectrum of 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials. The peaks located at 158.9 and 164.3eV corresponded to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+ in pure Bi2SiO5 sample, respectively (Fig. 3b). After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5, the peaks shift to 158.7 and 164.1eV, respectively, revealing the existence of interactions between CQDs and Bi2SiO5. Moreover, the peak at 103.2eV for Bi2SiO5 was corresponded to Si 2p of SiO5 6 − (Fig. 3c). As shown in Fig. 3d, the XPS signal of O1s derived from to the oxygen in Bi2SiO5 crystals. From the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3e), the signals at 284.8, 286.7, and 288.4eV demonstrated the presence of CC, oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon functional groups, respectively [28]. The XPS result was in good agree with the FT-IR analysis, suggesting the CQDs has been introduced to the Bi2SiO5.

Raman spectra of the as-prepared pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials were shown in Fig. S1. All the modes of SiO3 and Bi2O2 units are Raman active in the Bi2SiO5 crystal lattice, and their corresponding Raman peak positions are in good agreement with the literature values [10,32]. The peak at 168cm−1 was corresponding to the SiO mode. The peaks at 202, 302, 368 and 524cm−1 was derived from BiO mode [31]. After the CQDs was introduced to the Bi2SiO5, the peaks around 1393 and 1620cm−1 can be found which was attributed to the D-band and G-band of carbon [33]. The Raman result further indicated the introduction of CQDs to the Bi2SiO5.

The morphologies and microstructures of the Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 samples were characterized by SEM and TEM. The SEM image exhibited that the obtained Bi2SiO5 sample was nanosheets structure (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the pure Bi2SiO5 displayed the sheet-like structure with the thickness about 5nm, revealing the ultrathin nature of the obtained sample. Fig. 4b displayed the TEM image of 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 sample. It can be seen that several dark dots were dispersed on the surface of nanosheets. After the CQDs was introduced to the Bi2SiO5, the ultrathin structure of Bi2SiO5 can be maintained with CQDs dispersed on the surface. From the high-powered TEM image (Fig. 4c), the CQDs with average diameter of 3nm can be found to couple well with the Bi2SiO5. The above result suggested the CQDs has been modified on the Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets successfully. The content of CQDs was determined to be about 0.87wt% in the 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 sample by EDS analysis (Fig. 4d).

AFM analysis has been employed to further determine the thickness of the Bi2SiO5 sample. As shown in Fig. 5 , the AFM image and the corresponding height profile identified that the Bi2SiO5 sample displayed the ultrathin thickness about 4nm. To the best of our knowledge, the Bi2SiO5 nanosheets displayed the thinnest thickness reported up to now. The result of AFM analysis and TEM analysis revealing the ultrathin Bi2SiO5 materials can be controlled prepared via this synthetic method. Since the ultrathin materials should be prone to generate the defects in general, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum was employed to determine the existence of defects in the prepared Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets. As shown in the Fig. S3, no signal attributable to the defects can be observed, suggesting the defect was not the main factor to affect the photocatalytic performance of the obtained Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets in this system.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was employed to measure the optical bandgap and absorption capability of the obtained materials. As shown in Fig. 6 , the onset absorption band edge of Bi2SiO5 located at around 363nm, corresponding to band gap of 3.42eV. After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5, the absorption intensity within the 363–800nm region increased gradually. The higher the CQDs content was, the stronger absorption the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 exhibited and the darker the color of the samples. The modified CQDs was beneficial in increasing the light capture which may endow the formation of more electron-hole pairs.[34]

As is well-known, the larger specific surface area of photocatalyst could provide more surface active sites and could adsorb more pollutant on its surface, which would favor the improvement of photocatalytic activity [35]. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were carried out to determine the BET specific surface areas and porosity, as shown in Fig. 7 . The type IV isotherm featuring can be observed, suggesting the existence of mesopores (2–50nm) formed between Bi2SiO5 nanosheets [36]. All the samples displayed the similar BET specific surface areas (30.87, 30.76, 29.93 and 28.94m2/g for pure Bi2SiO5, 0.5wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5, 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 and 2wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5, respectively) and pore size distribution. Therefore, it can be concluded that the specific surface area was not the key factor to determine the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials in this system.

The charge separation was the most complex and key factor essentially determining the efficiency of photocatalysis. To investigate the charge carriers transfer efficiency of pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials, the transient photocurrent response curves were measured, as shown in Fig. 8 a. Despite the same BET specific surface area, the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials exhibited increased charge carrier separation efficiency than pure Bi2SiO5, which was determined from the photocurrent density. The higher photocurrent obtained by the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials could be assigned to electron transfer from the conduction band of Bi2SiO5 to CQDs, after which these electrons were localized in CQDs. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was in agreement with the above result. The CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials displayed much lower resistance than pure Bi2SiO5 sample, as evidenced by the reduced diameter of the semicircle in the high-frequency region in the EIS profiles (Fig. 8b). The highly conductive CQDs can facilitate electron transfer from Bi2SiO5 nanosheets within the whole electrode and thus decrease resistance [37].

Further insight into the electron transfer and separation behavior was revealed by steady-state PL spectra and fluorescence-lifetime experiments (Fig. 8c and d). After the modification of CQDs, the PL intensity degreased significantly and a greatly increase of the fluorescence lifetime can be observed, revealing the higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs can be achieved. The decay curves could be fitted well with a four-exponential function and the calculated average lifetime values were 6.39ns for pure Bi2SiO5 and 10.19ns for the 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials, respectively. The longer lifetime indicated that more electrons were transferred from Bi2SiO5 to CQDs across the interface since the conjugated π structure of CQDs and effective separation of charge carriers can be obtained [38].

Rhodamine B (RhB), as one typical pollutant model for evaluation of the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts, was employed to study the photocatalytic activities of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 under UV-light irradiation. The pure CQDs cannot display the photocatalytic activity for the pollutant degradation. As shown in Fig. 9 , 62.6% of RhB can be removed by pure Bi2SiO5 after irradiation for 4h. And the photocatalytic activity of pure Bi2SiO5 was similar to the Bi2SiO5 microspheres in literature [11]. After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5 nanosheets, it was obvious that RhB degraded faster in the presence of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials than in the presence of the pure Bi2SiO5, which derived from the improved photogenerated charge carrier separation of Bi2SiO5 by electron injection to CQDs. The 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 sample displayed the highest activity and 92.9% of RhB can be degraded at the same time. When the CQDs content was higher than 1wt%, a further increase of CQDs content could result in the decrease of photocatalytic activity. This was attributed to that although the modified CQDs could accelerate the charge transfer from the Bi2SiO5 to CQDs and lead to the effective separation of charge carriers, but too many CQDs cover on the surface of Bi2SiO5 would limit the light absorption of Bi2SiO5. Similar results can also been found in the literatures [39,40]. The CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials has been employed to degrade the phenol and the result was shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen that the 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials displayed improved photocatalytic activity than pure Bi2SiO5 materials for the degradation of phenol. Total organic carbon (TOC) experiment has been carried out for the degradation of phenol by 1wt% CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials (Fig. S5). After irradiation for 6h, 23.8% of phenol was mineralized, which indicating the phenol can be degraded by CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials under irradiation.

Most photodecomposition processes of organic pollutants directly utilize the produced holes in valence band with sufficient oxidative power, or use the formed other active species such as hydroxyl radical (
OH) and superoxide radical (O2
−). The
OH was resulted from the reaction of holes oxidize the surface adsorbed water or hydroxyl group. And the O2
− was formed via the single electron reduction of molecule oxygen [41]. The photocatalytic activities of the pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials were further estimated by monitoring the generation of O2
− and
OH, the important kind of active species for organic pollutant degradation. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were performed for pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials in methanol and water under UV light irradiation, respectively [42]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the characteristic signals of the DMPO-O2
− could be observed for the pure Bi2SiO5 sample under light irradiation, suggesting the O2
− can be generated efficiently via the single electron transfer process. Meanwhile, a fourfold characteristic peak with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 was observed for DMPO-
OH adduct [43], revealing the ·OH can also be generated for pure Bi2SiO5 (Fig. 10b). After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5, the signal of DMPO-O2
− and DMPO-
OH can also be found under light irradiation and the intensities were higher than that of pure Bi2SiO5 (Fig. 10c and d). It indicated that more O2
− and
OH can be produced by the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials, and can be inferred the higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials.

To get further insight into the generation of O2
− and
OH, the electron structure of Bi2SiO5 was investigated. In XPS analysis, the information on total density of states (DOS) of the valence band can be determined. The conduction band minimum (CBM) of Bi2SiO5 was primarily derived from the hybridization of the Bi 6s, O 2p and O 2s orbitals, while the Si 3p, O 2p, and Bi 5d states contribute to the valence band maximum (VBM) [14]. As shown in Fig. 11 , the highest occupied electronic density of states was measured to be 2.41eV for the obtained Bi2SiO5 material by XPS valence spectra. After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5, the similar result with about 2.41eV can be obtained. It indicated that the electronic density of states of Bi2SiO5 would not be affected by the CQDs. The CQDs existed in the form of surface modification for efficient transfer electrons without doping into the crystal lattice of Bi2SiO5. According to the formula E CB = E VB − E g and the E g obtained from the DRS analysis, the E CB of Bi2SiO5 can be calculated to be −1.01eV. Considering the E 0(
OH/OH−) was 2.38eV vs. NHE [44,45], the holes on the VB of Bi2SiO5 (2.41eV) could oxide the OH− to produce ·OH. At the same time, the electrons on the CB of Bi2SiO5 (−1.01eV) would reduce the molecule oxygen to O2
− via single electron transfer process as the E 0(O2/O2
−) was about −0.046eV [46]. After the CQDs was modified on the Bi2SiO5, the effective separation of electron-hole pairs spatially could boost the utilization of carriers to produce more active species. It was consistent with the ESR result.

According to the analysis aforementioned, the tentative photocatalytic reaction mechanism diagram over CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials was proposed in Fig. 12 . Upon light irradiation, the electrons were promoted from VB of Bi2SiO5 to its corresponding CB. Those photogenerated electrons would migrate to the surface of Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets and then transfer to CQDs across the interface between CQDs and Bi2SiO5. The electrons on the CQDs would reduce the adsorbed O2 to yield O2
−. And the holes on the VB of Bi2SiO5 could oxidize the OH− to produce
OH. The generated reactive oxygen species played important role in the subsequently photocatalytic degradation process.

CQDs modified Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets have been obtained for the first time. The CQDs were anchored on the surface of Bi2SiO5 ultrathin nanosheets and tight junctions were obtained. After being modified by CQDs, the CQDs/Bi2SiO5 displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under the UV light irradiation. The crucial role of CQDs for the enhanced photocatalytic performance was mainly originated from the excellent electron transfer ability of CQDs which enabled the effective separation of electron-hole pairs. The main active species were determined to be
OH and O2
− for both the pure Bi2SiO5 and CQDs/Bi2SiO5 materials. And the amount of active species can be increased by the CQDs modification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), and the Science and Technology Support Program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2016.05.009.
